title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Lewis Carter, who makes a local history podcast.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're meeting Lewis Carter, who makes a popular podcast about local history. Lewis, why did you start recording it? || Guest: I used to write short articles for a community newsletter, and people liked them. But [20]when I interviewed older residents, I realised their voices made the stories much more powerful than written words[/20]. That pushed me towards audio. || Interviewer: How do you choose your subjects? || Guest: I keep a list of ideas from listeners, but I don't use them all. [21]I choose stories that connect one person's memory with a place people can still visit today[/21]. That helps listeners picture it clearly. || Interviewer: What was difficult at the beginning? || Guest: I thought editing would be the hardest part, and it did take practice. However, [22]the real challenge was learning how to ask questions without interrupting people too much[/22]. Silence can be useful in an interview. || Interviewer: Has anything surprised you about your listeners? || Guest: Yes. I imagined most listeners would be retired people who already knew the area. [23]In fact, many are young families who have recently moved here and want to understand the town[/23]. That's been lovely. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most when making an episode? || Guest: I like finding old photographs, but [24]my favourite moment is when someone remembers a small detail they thought nobody else would care about[/24]. Those details bring history to life. || Interviewer: What are your plans for the podcast? || Guest: At the moment, I release one episode a month. [25]Next year I hope to make a short series about the river, because it has shaped the town for centuries[/25]. There are lots of stories there. || Interviewer: Do you ever worry about getting facts wrong? || Guest: All the time. Memories are important, but they can be different from official records. I check dates in the library and ask other people to confirm details. If I'm not sure, I say so in the episode. Listeners appreciate honesty more than pretending to know everything. || Interviewer: Has making the podcast changed how you feel about your town? || Guest: Very much. I notice buildings and street names I walked past for years without thinking. It has made the town feel richer, and it has also made me more patient. Everyone has a story if you give them enough time to tell it. || Interviewer: How do you record interviews with older residents? || Guest: I usually visit them at home because they feel more comfortable there. I take a small microphone, but I try not to make the equipment seem important. We often talk for half an hour before recording properly, so they can relax. || Interviewer: Have you ever changed your opinion after making an episode? || Guest: Yes. I once thought an old factory was just an ugly building, but after hearing how many families depended on it, I saw it differently. The podcast has taught me that places can matter even when they are not beautiful. || Interviewer: Do you use music in the podcast? || Guest: Only a little. I want the voices to be the main thing, so I use short pieces at the start and end. Too much music would make the stories feel less natural, and listeners have told me they prefer a simple style.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're meeting Lewis Carter, who makes a popular podcast about local history. Lewis, why did you start recording it?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I used to write short articles for a community newsletter, and people liked them. But when I interviewed older residents, I realised their voices made the stories much more powerful than written words. That pushed me towards audio.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you choose your subjects?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I keep a list of ideas from listeners, but I don't use them all. I choose stories that connect one person's memory with a place people can still visit today. That helps listeners picture it clearly.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult at the beginning?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I thought editing would be the hardest part, and it did take practice. However, the real challenge was learning how to ask questions without interrupting people too much. Silence can be useful in an interview.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has anything surprised you about your listeners?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes. I imagined most listeners would be retired people who already knew the area. In fact, many are young families who have recently moved here and want to understand the town. That's been lovely.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most when making an episode?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I like finding old photographs, but my favourite moment is when someone remembers a small detail they thought nobody else would care about. Those details bring history to life.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are your plans for the podcast?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: At the moment, I release one episode a month. Next year I hope to make a short series about the river, because it has shaped the town for centuries. There are lots of stories there.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you ever worry about getting facts wrong?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: All the time. Memories are important, but they can be different from official records. I check dates in the library and ask other people to confirm details. If I'm not sure, I say so in the episode. Listeners appreciate honesty more than pretending to know everything.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has making the podcast changed how you feel about your town?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Very much. I notice buildings and street names I walked past for years without thinking. It has made the town feel richer, and it has also made me more patient. Everyone has a story if you give them enough time to tell it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you record interviews with older residents?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I usually visit them at home because they feel more comfortable there. I take a small microphone, but I try not to make the equipment seem important. We often talk for half an hour before recording properly, so they can relax.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you ever changed your opinion after making an episode?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes. I once thought an old factory was just an ugly building, but after hearing how many families depended on it, I saw it differently. The podcast has taught me that places can matter even when they are not beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you use music in the podcast?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Only a little. I want the voices to be the main thing, so I use short pieces at the start and end. Too much music would make the stories feel less natural, and listeners have told me they prefer a simple style.

questions.question: Why did Lewis decide to make a podcast?

options.A: He found spoken stories more powerful than written ones.
options.B: He wanted to stop writing for a newsletter.
options.C: He was asked to record interviews for school.

questions.explanation: Lewis says older residents’ voices made the stories more powerful than written words.

questions.question: What kind of stories does Lewis choose?

options.A: Stories suggested by the oldest listeners.
options.B: Stories linked to places people can visit.
options.C: Stories about famous people from the town.

questions.explanation: He chooses stories that connect a memory with a place people can still visit.

questions.question: What did Lewis find most difficult at first?
questions.explanation: He says the real challenge was asking questions without interrupting people too much.

questions.question: What surprised Lewis about his listeners?

options.A: Many are new young families in the town.
options.B: Most are retired people from the area.

questions.explanation: Lewis says many listeners are young families who have recently moved to the town.

questions.question: What does Lewis enjoy most when making an episode?

options.C: Reading official records in the library.

questions.explanation: He says his favourite moment is when someone remembers a small detail.

questions.question: What does Lewis plan to do next year?

options.C: Produce a series about the river.

questions.explanation: He says he hopes to make a short series about the river.
